exemplar.title: To what extent does sleep deprivation affect cognitive functioning?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does sleep deprivation affect cognitive functioning?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, focused and consistently revisited.
Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with a logical structure and clear purpose.
Methodology demonstrates a reasonable range of relevant sources and some evidence of informed selection.
weaknesses: Scope remains broad, covering multiple cognitive domains without always re-anchoring to the central question.
Method description lacks an explicit roadmap and justification for source inclusion/exclusion.
Occasional drift into material (e.g., reward circuitry) that isnât tightly linked back to the research question.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs introduction and title block present a precise research question and effective rationale, and the essayâs headings and table of contents reflect a coherent plan. However, the scope remains somewhat over-ambitious and there is no fully explicit methodology statement describing how sources were selected. Performance aligns with the 5-6 band in communication and focus but dips slightly on methodological transparency, warranting 5/6.

strengths: Demonstrates good subject knowledge of sleep physiology and cognitive systems.
Applies key empirical studies appropriately to illustrate each cognitive domain.
Uses psychological terminology (e.g. memory consolidation, hyperalgesia) accurately and consistently.
weaknesses: Explanations are often descriptive rather than integrative, lacking deeper conceptual synthesis.
Informal phrasing and occasional undefined acronyms (e.g. TSD) undermine academic precision.
Sections omit critical theoretical frameworks (e.g. circadian vs. homeostatic influences) and primary neuroscience citations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows solid knowledge and generally accurate use of terminology, situating it in the âgoodâ 3-4 band. Persistent descriptive passages, informal language, and missing citations prevent placement in the highest band, so 4/6 is appropriate.

strengths: Selection of peer-reviewed literature is largely relevant to the research question, covering multiple methodologies.
Analysis compares effect sizes and methodological strengths/weaknesses, and draws useful real-world parallels (e.g., fatigue vs. intoxication).
Reasoned argument acknowledges counter-evidence and situates findings within applied contexts (e.g., high-risk professions).
weaknesses: Synthesis across cognitive domains is uneven, with limited integration of quantitative data (e.g., statistical thresholds, lapse rates).
Discussion remains partially descriptive and lacks fully critical evaluation of ecological validity and generalizability.
Some evaluations (e.g. selection bias, age effects) are noted but not quantitatively assessed or weighed in overall conclusions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work demonstrates good research relevance, clear analysis, and a reasoned argument, placing it in the 7-9 band for Critical Thinking. However, gaps in synthesis, depth of critical evaluation, and quantitative integration prevent entry into the top band, yielding 8/12.

strengths: Structure meets EE conventions: title page, abstract-style introduction, table of contents, section headings and pagination are all present.
Layout and formatting (word count, page numbers, consistent citation style) are correctly applied.
Transitions between major sections are clear, enhancing readability.
weaknesses: Minor typographical inconsistencies (e.g. mixed italics in sub-headings) and uneven spacing are present.
Some paragraphing choices and overly long sentences impede flow and clarity.
In-text formatting of quoted technical terms lacks full consistency.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All required presentation elements are correctly implemented with only minor stylistic and formatting inconsistencies. This aligns with the highest band for Presentation (3-4), so the essay merits 4/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: Psychological terminology such as "memory consolidation," "hyperalgesia," "top-down attention" and "psychomotor vigilance" is employed accurately and consistently.  Concepts are generally defined before use and applied appropriately in later discussion.  Minor lapses occur when everyday language (e.g., "our nerves that create memories") substitutes for precise neuroscientific phrasing, preventing placement in the highest band, but overall usage is mostly correct.

The essay contains a word count, title, page numbers and Harvard-style references.  Tables, headings and line spacing are consistently formatted, and in-text citations follow a uniform style.  A few typographical inconsistencies (e.g., mixed use of italics in sub-headings) are present but minor.  Overall, required layout and formatting elements are present and correctly applied.

The student demonstrates solid knowledge of sleep physiology and cognitive systems, accurately summarising hippocampal encoding, working memory circuitry and vigilance mechanisms.  Key empirical studies are applied appropriately to illustrate each cognitive domain, and basic neuroanatomical terms (PFC, PPC, SMA) are used correctly.  Some explanations remain descriptive rather than integrative, and the literature base is weighted toward classic rather than cutting-edge research, so the work sits firmly in the âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ band.

The essay relies on secondary research and draws on a reasonable range of peer-reviewed studies from 1994â2010, showing some informed selection.  Strengths include brief critical comments on sample size, ecological validity and generalisability after each study.  Nevertheless, there is no explicit explanation of how sources were located, nor any clear justification of inclusion/exclusion criteria.  The methodology therefore appears mostly complete but not systematic, aligning with the mid-band description.

The student presents a precise, clearly-worded research question in the title block and restates it in the introduction.  The opening paragraphs explain why the topic matters (prevalence of curtailed sleep, risks for high-stakes professions) and outline which cognitive domains will be assessed.  Headings and a table of contents signal a logical plan.  Minor weaknesses remain: the scope ("cognitive functioning" in general) is ambitious and occasionally drifts into multiple constructs without always re-anchoring them to the guiding question.  Nevertheless, the overall purpose and focus are communicated accurately and effectively.

The literature cited is largely peer-reviewed and directly relevant to the RQ, covering experimental, neuroimaging and epidemiological perspectives.  Source selection spans different cognitive domains, demonstrating an adequate breadth.  Some gaps remainârecent findings on chronic partial sleep loss are absent and the reliance on older, homogeneous samples is notedâbut overall the research base is appropriate and mostly pertinent, meriting the top of the 4-6 band.

The research question is stated verbatim and revisited in the conclusion, showing a consistent thread.  However, it remains quite broad, encompassing attention, memory, executive function, decision making and pain perception.  The body chapters do not always justify why each chosen sub-domain is essential to answering the overarching "to what extent" query, so the focus is only partially sharp.  Overall the question is clear but not fully delimited, placing it comfortably in the 3-4 band.

A title page, abstract-style introduction, logical section headings, table of contents and reference list are all present, meeting EE structural conventions.  Pagination is correct and transitions between major sections are clear.  Minor issuesâsuch as occasional heading duplication and uneven spacingâdo not impede clarity, so the structure is clearly appropriate.

The discussion summarises principal findings and notes implications for high-risk professions, demonstrating a reasoned argument.  Evaluation of individual studies is present, yet integration into a coherent, critical stance on the overall extent of cognitive impact is limited.  Counter-evidence (e.g., sensory domains less affected) is acknowledged but not fully weighed.  Thus the evaluation is partially critical rather than fully analytical, corresponding to the lower end of the 4-6 band.

Analysis moves beyond description by comparing effect sizes, noting methodological strengths/weaknesses, and relating findings to real-world groups.  The student, for example, contrasts hippocampal under-activation with thalamic compensation and parallels fatigue with alcohol intoxication.  However, synthesis between studies is uneven; few explicit links are drawn across domains to judge the overall magnitude of impairment, and some evaluations remain surface-level.  The analysis is clear and mostly supported, aligning with the middle of the 4-6 band.

annotations.comment: The opening sentence is a fragment and lacks context; ensure each paragraph begins with a clear topic sentence that relates back to your argument.

The description of hippocampal function is overly descriptive without citing a primary neuroscience reference. Include a source (e.g.
Diekelmann & Born, 2010) when detailing underlying mechanisms.
You note the sampleâs all-male composition but do not discuss how this selection bias may limit generalizability. Elaborate on its potential impact on your conclusions.
The transition from sensory perception to working memory is abrupt; insert a linking sentence to clearly show how each cognitive domain addresses the central question.
The summary of Yoo et al. (2007) omits statistical thresholds and significance values used in the fMRI analysis. Including these details would enhance your evaluation of validity.
The introduction provides background on sleep but does not preview the methodology or explicitly link these points to the research question; add a concise roadmap of methods.

Informal phrasing such as âour nerves that create memoriesâ weakens academic precision. Use accurate neuroscientific terminology (e.g.
"neuronal synaptic consolidation") to strengthen subject knowledge.
The detailed discussion of reward circuitry drifts from the central question. Re-anchor these findings by explicitly stating how they inform the extent of cognitive impairment.

The research question is clearly stated, demonstrating strong focus, but it remains very broad by encompassing attention, memory, decision making, and sensory perception. Consider narrowing the scope to strengthen the link between your question and the depth of analysis.
The word count is provided and falls within the 4000-word limit, satisfying formatting requirements and demonstrating attention to report guidelines.
The table of contents is clear and logically organized, providing an effective roadmap that enhances navigability and structure of the essay.
The main heading repeats the research question verbatim; consider refining it to reflect your specific investigative focus and scope for greater precision.
Identifying selection bias is good, but you do not discuss how it might quantitatively alter results. Estimate the potential impact to strengthen evaluation.
A paragraph break before the methods description would improve readability. Consider dividing the text into subsections such as âParticipants,â âProcedure,â and âData Analysis.â
This sentence is overly long and complex, which may confuse readers. Split into two sentences to improve clarity and flow.
The acronym TSD appears without definition on this page. Ensure that each section defines technical terms at first use to support reader comprehension.
The text describing results could be supplemented with a table of effect sizes and p-values to present data more clearly and concisely.
Mentioning ecological validity is valuable, yet the discussion remains descriptive. Critically assess how tightly controlled lab results translate to real-world sleep deprivation scenarios.
The claim that the prefrontal cortex is âmost vulnerableâ requires citation of comparative data from other cortical regions; integrate such evidence to substantiate this statement.
The section on attention and vigilance omits analysis of circadian versus homeostatic influences. Integrate these theoretical frameworks to deepen your conceptual understanding.
Mentioning the time-on-task effect without quantitative values weakens rigor. Include lapse rates or reaction-time changes from cited studies to support your analysis.
You transition into pain thresholds without synthesizing how these results integrate with memory and attention findings. Add a sentence comparing domain-specific effects for coherence.
You integrate results from multiple age studies but do not compare effect sizes or magnitudes. Synthesize numerical findings to clarify how age modulates vigilance decrements.
Quotation marks for âtop-down attentionâ and âbottom-up attentionâ are inconsistently applied. Standardize formatting for all quoted technical terms.
The year of publication for Dawson and Reid is inconsistently cited as 1977 in text but listed as 1997 in references. Verify and correct this discrepancy.
The method description of Kundermann et al. is thorough but remains descriptive. Link procedural details back to how findings address your central research question.
